intro: Ruby and Python share a philosophy of developer happiness and readable syntax, making this one of the more natural conversions. Ruby's blocks and `yield` map closely to Python's first-class functions and decorators, Ruby's `Enumerable` methods (`map`, `select`, `reduce`) have direct Python equivalents, and Ruby's `nil` becomes Python's `None`. Ruby's `snake_case` convention is shared, though Ruby uses `end` keywords where Python uses indentation.

diffs.from: Blocks (do..end or {}) are anonymous closures passed to methods implicitly.
diffs.to: No blocks. Use lambda, closures via def, or comprehensions.

diffs.from: end keyword closes blocks, methods, classes. No braces.
diffs.to: Indentation defines blocks. No end keyword.

diffs.from: :symbol literals are interned strings. Used for hash keys and method refs.
diffs.to: No symbol type. Use plain str for hash keys. Strings are interned by Python automatically.
Built-in list methods + list/dict/set comprehensions. functools for fold.

diffs.from: require/require_relative for loading. Gems via Bundler + Gemfile.

examples.title: Ruby block → Python lambda / comprehension
examples.description: Ruby's block-based Enumerable methods map to Python comprehensions and built-ins.
examples.toCode:
numbers = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8]

squared = [n ** 2 for n in numbers if n % 2 == 0]
total   = sum(squared)

print(total)  # 120

examples.title: Ruby class → Python class
examples.description: Ruby's attr_accessor maps to plain Python properties. end keyword disappears entirely.

tips.title: Blocks → lambdas or def functions
tips.body: Ruby blocks { |x| x * 2 } become Python lambdas: lambda x: x * 2. Multi-line blocks with logic become named def functions passed as arguments.

tips.title: attr_accessor → plain attributes
tips.body: Ruby's attr_accessor :name generates getter/setter. Python uses self.name directly — no accessor macro needed. Use @property only for validation.

tips.title: Symbol keys → string keys
tips.body: :symbol hash keys become plain string keys in Python dicts: { name: "Alice" } → {"name": "Alice"}. The behavior is identical since Python interns short strings.

tips.title: Enumerable → comprehensions + builtins
tips.body: .select → list comprehension with if. .map → list comprehension or map(). .reduce → functools.reduce() or sum(). .each → for loop.
